title: Wave Resistance for Stochastic Motion at Interfaces
authors: Maxence Arutkin, Shlomi Reuveni, Elie Raphael
abstract: Wave resistance is the drag generated by the wave radiation that a source moving at a fluid interface sustains. Under stochastic trajectories, the mean drag is controlled by the ensemble-averaged surface profile built from the trajectory history. We show that the result is a finite resistance below the deterministic radiation threshold and a regularization of the singular response at the minimum phase velocity of the capillary-gravity waves. We derive explicit scaling laws for drifted Brownian trajectories, including a universal high-diffusivity decay. For drifted Lévy flight, we find the mean wave resistance in closed-form, extending wave-drag theory to non-Gaussian trajectories.
summary: This paper calculates the mean wave resistance generated by a source moving stochastically at a fluid interface. By incorporating diffusion into classical wave drag theory, the authors derive universal scaling laws that regularize singularities predicted in deterministic models. The results show how noise fundamentally alters the energy dissipation characteristics of the system.
main_problem: The research aims to determine the mean wave resistance (drag) experienced by a source moving stochastically at a fluid interface, extending classical deterministic theories.
main_result: The mean wave resistance exhibits distinct scaling laws depending on the diffusivity ($\Delta$) and drift speed ($v$), notably showing universal high-diffusivity decay $R \sim \Delta^{-5/3}$ and regularization of singularities near critical velocities.
method: The analysis employs Fourier transform methods, ensemble averaging over stochastic trajectories (Brownian motion, Lévy flights), and extensive asymptotic analysis to derive scaling laws in different parameter regimes.
model_or_system: The system models capillary-gravity waves radiating from a localized pressure source moving along an air–water interface. The dynamics are governed by linearized free-surface boundary conditions for incompressible, inviscid flow.
key_observables: Mean wave resistance $\langle R angle$, mean surface profile $\langle\eta(X)angle$.
important_parameters: Dimensionless diffusivity ($\Delta$), drift speed ($v$), critical minimum phase velocity ($c_{\min}$), and the deviation from threshold ($\delta = v-1$).
main_assumptions: Assumes incompressible, inviscid flow and small-amplitude deformations. The analysis is performed in one dimension by reducing the 2D framework.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate mean-profile asymmetry comparing deterministic vs. diffusive cases; plots show $R(v, \Delta)$ across various diffusivities, demonstrating crossovers between different scaling regimes.
paper_structure: The paper develops from a general integral formulation for wave resistance to systematically derive asymptotic forms by applying limiting approximations corresponding to high diffusivity ($\Delta \gg 1$), near-threshold behavior ($v \approx c_{\min}$), and low velocity limits.
why_interesting: While focused on fluid dynamics, the mathematical techniques—especially the use of ensemble averaging over stochastic processes (Brownian motion) to regularize singular deterministic responses—are highly relevant to open quantum systems where noise or environmental coupling smooths out sharp theoretical predictions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Mean wave resistance versus drift speed across diffusivities. Wave resistance R(v, ∆) versus dimensionless drift speed v for Brownian motion with Lorentzian pressure field (a = 0.20). Solid curves: exact evaluation of Eq. (3) for sixteen diffusivities ∆∈[10−6, 102]. Red dashed line: classical Havelock resistance (v > 1), recovered as ∆→0. The corresponding mean surface profiles are displayed in the Fig. S1 in the Supplemental Material.

FIG. 1. Mean-profile asymmetry. Mean surface ⟨η(X)⟩in the comoving frame at v = 0.8 for ∆= 0 (deterministic, sym- metric, no drag) and ∆= 1 (diffusive, skewed, finite drag). Pressure P0 shaded in gray. Lorentzian source, a = 0.20.

FIG. 4. Threshold behavior near v = 1. Left: Mean resistance R(v, ∆) in the threshold region for nine diffusiv- ities, showing the peak at v = 1 and Regime III asymp- totics. Right: Same data plotted against the reduced variable x = δ/∆using the rescaled observable Y = (2/π) e2a √

FIG. 3. Asymptotic scaling laws across diffusivity regimes. Mean wave resistance R(v, ∆) versus diffusivity ∆for five drift speeds v ∈[0.6, 3.0] (a = 0.20, log-log axes). Solid curves: exact evaluation of Eq. (3). At high diffusivities, all curves align sharing the universal ∼∆−5/3 asymptotics of Eq. (5) (Regime I). At low diffusivities, and at subcriti- cal velocities, one obtains the ∼∆asymptotics of Eq. (6) (Regime II), while at critical speed v = 1, one obtains the ∼∆−1/2 asymptotics of Eq. (7) evaluated at δ = 0. At low diffusivities, and supercritical velocities, one approaches the Havelock limit which is insensitive to ∆.
